text.title: hybrid-search Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for hybrid-search. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Use Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) as the default. Formula: RRF_score(d) = SUM(1 / (k + rank_i(d))), k=60. No score normalization needed; works across different score scales.
rule.rationale: RRF ignores actual score magnitudes and only uses rank positions. This makes it robust when BM25 and vector scores are on completely different scales, which is always the case without normalization.

rule.statement: Use linear combination (hybrid_score = alpha * vector + (1-alpha) * bm25) only when RRF is measurably worse on your query set and you have the capacity to normalize and tune. Default alpha=0.7 (semantic-leaning).
rule.rationale: Linear combination provides fine-grained control but requires min-max or z-score normalization of both signals. Without normalization, BM25's unbounded scores dominate entirely.

rule.statement: Apply query-adaptive alpha: quoted phrases → 0.2; product/ref codes → 0.3; short queries → 0.5; natural language → 0.7.
rule.rationale: A fixed alpha is suboptimal for mixed query distributions. A lightweight classifier adds negligible overhead but significantly improves precision across query types.

rule.statement: Rerank top-100 candidates (not top-10). Fetch 2-3x the final k for fusion, rerank the pool, then return top-10. A reranker given too few candidates cannot recover good results that ranked poorly in either retriever.
rule.rationale: Reranking adds ~50-100ms and 5x cost compared to retrieval alone. Only worth enabling when precision matters more than latency. Common rerankers: Cohere Rerank (API), bge-reranker-v2-m3 (self-hosted).

rule.statement: Use native hybrid search endpoints (Weaviate alpha, Qdrant FusionQuery) over manual two-call fusion. Native: lower latency, fewer failure modes, less code.
rule.rationale: Manual fusion requires two separate network calls, two failure modes, and custom RRF implementation. Native endpoints do all of this in a single API call.

rule.statement: Set BM25 parameters for your content: short document chunks need b=0.3-0.5; long documents need b=0.75; technical content needs k1=1.5-2.0; general content k1=1.2. Match BM25 tokenizer to corpus language.
rule.rationale: Default BM25 parameters (k1=1.5, b=0.75) are calibrated for long English prose. Applying them to short chunks or code degrades keyword matching significantly.

rule.statement: Log retriever attribution: which retriever contributed each successful result. After 2 weeks, if BM25 contributes less than 10% of final results, hybrid may not be justified for that query distribution.
rule.rationale: Hybrid adds complexity and ~20% latency. If your query distribution is purely semantic, the BM25 component is waste. Data-driven decisions about disabling hybrid prevent unnecessary complexity.

rule.statement: Before switching from pure vector to hybrid in production, A/B test on 5% of traffic and measure a quality metric. Latency + complexity cost must be justified by measurable gain.
rule.rationale: Hybrid is not always better. On conversational or creative corpora, BM25 can add noise. Measure on real traffic, not on a benchmark that may not reflect your query distribution.

rule.statement: For mixed queries, the agent runs BM25 and vector search in parallel, applies RRF fusion, and optionally passes the top-100 candidates through a cross-encoder reranker to get the final top-10.
rule.rationale: This approach maximizes recall and precision while keeping latency bounded.

rule.statement: The agent logs which retriever contributed to each final result for monitoring and tuning.
rule.rationale: This data drives decisions about whether hybrid search is still necessary and whether the cost is justified.
